title: The Moral of Rebuild
wikitext:
[]: The Moral of Rebuild             Quote Post by Chuckman » Fri Aug 23, 2013 9:31 am [Split from "How is Shinji responsible for 3rd Impact?" . - Monk] The nail that sticks up will get hammered down. Rebuild, so far , is a heavily conformist narrative. Shinji is almost a villain because he refuses to just shut up and do what he's told. The moral of this story, so far , has been "do what you're told". He's heroic when he does what he's told (get in the robot and kill the giant diamond, Shinji) and causes tragedy when he doesn't (kill the Eva and turn your friend-with-a-crush into a smear on your fist, Shinji, the rest of the world is more important than her). What many fail to see is that N3I is a sequel, in the scene-and-sequence sense, to the Bardiel battle. In both instances, Shinji places the worth of another human being over the rest of humanity (first by refusing to kill an angel, then by killing the shit out of it ) Again it's hammered home that he should just do what he's told. He neither needs nor deserves an explanation; he should accept the word of his elders at face value and when he doesn't, it leads to further suffering. Shinji sinned, died, and woke up in a twisted, torturous version of the world he used to know- he's in hell. We can't say what this will ultimately lead to until Final comes out. This whole topic comes down to cultural dissonance. If you're a member of the intended audience, Shinji is a shameful denier of responsibility. If you're looking at him from a Western perspective, you see a grim-faced and misguided hero with a selfless, determined streak who's had the universe take a giant shit on him out of cosmic/authorial pettiness.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Fri Aug 23, 2013 9:36 am Chuckman wrote: Rebuild, so far , is a heavily conformist narrative. Shinji is almost a villain because he refuses to just shut up and do what he's told. The moral of this story, so far , has been "do what you're told". He's heroic when he does what he's told (get in the robot and kill the giant diamond, Shinji) and causes tragedy when he doesn't (kill the Eva and turn your friend-with-a-crush into a smear on your fist, Shinji, the rest of the world is more important than her).

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Fri Aug 23, 2013 9:48 am Because you gotta fight the power, man. That kind of mental calculus is morally dubious at best. Remember that anyone else giving him orders might have pointed out that fighting back was the only chance at saving her; Misato's absence during the fight serves the important purpose of putting Gendo in a position to offer Shinji a choice between "fuck that bitch do what I say" and "go limp". He's a kid and he's in a really fucked up situation, and he's basically untrained, it's not reasonable to expect him to make a level headed decision with that kind of pressure even before you add in the conflict with his father. Shinji actually has a great deal of fortitude for refusing the order. In a similar situation, most people will what the authority figure tells them, especially if that authority figure absolves them of responsibility. It's also a pretty common narrative convention for the protagonist to fight a possessed or compromised comrade and save them anyway. Eva is all about being anti-wish-fulfillment, so that option is off the table.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Fri Aug 23, 2013 10:20 am Chuckman wrote: Remember that anyone else giving him orders might have pointed out that fighting back was the only chance at saving her; Misato's absence during the fight serves the important purpose of putting Gendo in a position to offer Shinji a choice between "fuck that bitch do what I say" and "go limp". He's a kid and he's in a really fucked up situation, and he's basically untrained, it's not reasonable to expect him to make a level headed decision with that kind of pressure even before you add in the conflict with his father.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Fri Aug 23, 2013 10:40 am The question is not whether his options are limited, but whether the options he could think of were limited. It's easy to sit back and say he could have done this or that, but could he reasonably be expected to conceive of a plan under those conditions? You're not talking about a seasoned combat veteran with training here. He's a kid in an almost unfathomable situation. Fighter pilots don't have to worry that they'll have to shoot down their wingman because their airplane is possessed by alien goo.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Fri Aug 23, 2013 10:43 am Chuckman wrote: The question is not whether his options are limited, but whether the options he could think of were limited. It's easy to sit back and say he could have done this or that, but could he reasonably be expected to conceive of a plan under those conditions? You're not talking about a seasoned combat veteran with training here. He's a kid in an almost unfathomable situation. Fighter pilots don't have to worry that they'll have to shoot down their wingman because their airplane is possessed by alien goo.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Fri Aug 23, 2013 10:55 am Reluctance to hurt another person is wrong?

[]:             Quote Post by Conspicuous » Fri Aug 23, 2013 11:05 am If a reluctance to hurt them will lead to certain death for them, than yes, I would say it's wrong to put your own morals over their safety.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Fri Aug 23, 2013 11:31 am It's easy to think that way when you're not in the situation. He was under every kind of pressure Shinji Ikari could be under at that moment.

[]:             Quote Post by Conspicuous » Fri Aug 23, 2013 11:34 am Oh, I'm not saying that it was reasonable to expect him to do better, but even so, it's still a mistake, and the most important part of mistakes is learning from the ordeal. After all, we all know that Shinji can and should do better than that.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Fri Aug 23, 2013 11:59 am If Shinji was really concerned about the person in the Eva he would have been trying to come up with something to save her. Instead he just doubled down and said "no no no, you can't make me!" and refused to budge, circumstances and consequences be damned. So yeah, refusing to hurt someone else when it'll wind up killing the person is wrong. When that will also result in the extinction of every life on Earth it's double-plus wrong. Not that this has anything to do with his starting 3I, of course.

[]:             Quote Post by Stillborn » Fri Aug 23, 2013 12:18 pm You forgot that he decided he would rather be killed himself than kill another person.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Fri Aug 23, 2013 12:21 pm Also that nobody was talking about saving her. Gendo specifically told him to kill her. He could have pointed out that the only way to save her would be to fight back, but it either didn't occur to him, or it did and he didn't care.

[]:             Quote Post by KingXanaduu » Fri Aug 23, 2013 12:30 pm I think a lot of his reasoning for his refusal to act is because he doesn't want that kind of blood on his hands. But, News Flash, he's a soldier, and he's gonna have blood on his hands whether he likes it or not. However, the problem is that NOBODY bothered to prepare him psychologically for this and they just expect him to deal with it and walk it off, which isn't very easy for a person like Shinji to suddenly have to kill a person he cares about for the Greater Good. It's amazing how societies work like that, like Nerv and Seele. "You're going to have to do acts that will go against your moral standings, but it's all for the Greater Good, so deal with it."

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Fri Aug 23, 2013 12:37 pm Stillborn wrote: You forgot that he decided he would rather be killed himself than kill another person.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Fri Aug 23, 2013 12:58 pm Bagheera wrote: That doesn't mean much given his suicidal tendencies. I don't see how that has anything to do with Shinji's stated motives. He still wasn't trying to come up with any alternatives on his own, and unlike in NGE there's no question that he knew the stakes this time (which might have been the point to the scene with Lilith in 1.xx, come to think of it). And of course none of this is commentary on Gendo's epic fail during that sequence. It's indisputable, but irrelevant when looking at Shinji's failures. The fact the one failed doesn't mean the other didn't.

[]:             Quote Post by Conspicuous » Fri Aug 23, 2013 1:02 pm I'm still going to argue that his biggest failure wasn't that he failed to make the right decision under the extreme pressure that he was under. I mean, honestly, lets be fair on the kid here, that's a shitload of pressure and to expect him to come up with a better plan is just stretching it. I will argue, however, that the real problem is that he failed to learn anything from it even after the event had passed. Life is a long series of mistakes, some small, some big, but the only way to fail is by not learning and growing from the event. N3I happened when he shut himself out of the outside world entirely, causing him to fail to notice that the world was burning because of his own desires. I doubt he would've gone through with it if he was actually aware of what was happening, but since he wasn't paying attention, the Eva ended up doing its thing and initiate the end of humanity. Of course, this is funnier when you remember that when he first met Shikinami, she tripped him to see if he had any sort of awareness of his surroundings. Guess he didn't learn from the event.

[]:             Quote Post by Charsi » Fri Aug 23, 2013 1:08 pm Chuckman wrote: Shinji actually has a great deal of fortitude for refusing the order. In a similar situation, most people will what the authority figure tells them, especially if that authority figure absolves them of responsibility.

[]:             Quote Post by KingXanaduu » Fri Aug 23, 2013 1:17 pm Charsi wrote: Just a tiny dose of eagle eye hindsight here... :-/ Shinji, at the time of making the decision, had no reason to believe Asuka would survive his defeat of the Angel. Every Angel he's seen defeated until that point has vaporized into a shower of blood and a rainbow. What reason does he have to believe this would behave any differently just because it's an infected Eva? It's actually somewhat amazing there are recoverable pieces of the Eva after all is said and done. Every other Angel has left nothing behind for analysis. He's also confronted by a situation where nobody even mentions any effort to save the pilot. It's a rather large idiot ball. Imagine if Gendo had instead said "damage the target until it becomes inactive and we will make every effort to retrieve the pilot". But that's not what the plot required ;)

[]:             Quote Post by Stillborn » Fri Aug 23, 2013 1:29 pm In other word: Learn to be a martyr for us and don't complain.
